corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: one of the primary goals of the field of artificial intelligence is to solve complex tasks from unprocessed high-dimensional sensory input recently significant progress has been made by combining advances in deep learning for sensory processing with reinforcement learning resulting in the deep q network dqn algorithm that is capable of human level performance on many atari video games using unprocessed pixels for input to do so deep neural network function approximators were used to estimate the action-value function however while dqn solves problems with high-dimensional observation spaces it can only handle discrete and low-dimensional action spaces many tasks of interest most notably physical control tasks have continuous real valued and high dimensional action spaces dqn cannot be straightforwardly applied to continuous domains since it relies on a finding the action that maximizes the action-value function which in the continuous valued case requires an iterative optimization process at every step an obvious approach to adapting deep reinforcement learning

context_window.previous: we adapt the ideas underlying the success of deep q-learning to the continuous action domain we present an actor-critic model-free algorithm based on the deterministic policy gradient that can operate over continuous action spaces using the same learning algorithm network architecture and hyper-parameters our algorithm robustly solves more than 20 simulated physics tasks including classic problems such as cartpole swing-up dexterous manipulation legged locomotion and car driving our algorithm is able to find policies whose performance is competitive with those found by a planning algorithm with full access to the dynamics of the domain and its derivatives we further demonstrate that for many of the tasks the algorithm can learn policies end-to-end directly from raw pixel inputs body

top_candidates.sentence: Instead, here we used an actor-critic approach based on the DPG algorithm .
The DPG algorithm maintains a parameterized actor function µ(s|θ µ ) which specifies the current policy by deterministically mapping states to a specific action.
Because DDPG is an off-policy algorithm, the replay buffer can be large, allowing the algorithm to benefit from learning across a set of uncorrelated transitions.
An advantage of offpolicies algorithms such as DDPG is that we can treat the problem of exploration independently from the learning algorithm.
We refer to our algorithm as Deep DPG (DDPG, Algorithm 1).
We present an actor-critic, model-free algorithm based on the deterministic policy gradient that can operate over continuous action spaces.
We present an actor-critic, model-free algorithm based on the deterministic policy gradient that can operate over continuous action spaces.

Using the same learning algorithm, network architecture and hyper-parameters, our algorithm robustly solves more than 20 simulated physics tasks, including classic problems such as cartpole swing-up, dexterous manipulation, legged locomotion and car driving.

Using the same learning algorithm, network architecture and hyper-parameters, our algorithm robustly solves more than 20 simulated physics tasks, including classic problems such as cartpole swing-up, dexterous manipulation, legged locomotion and car driving.
Our algorithm is able to find policies whose performance is competitive with those found by a planning algorithm with full access to the dynamics of the domain and its derivatives.
We further demonstrate that for many of the tasks the algorithm can learn policies "end-to-end": directly from raw pixel inputs.

Recently, significant progress has been made by combining advances in deep learning for sensory processing with reinforcement learning, resulting in the "Deep Q Network" (DQN) algorithm that is capable of human level performance on many Atari video games using unprocessed pixels for input.

DQN cannot be straightforwardly applied to continuous domains since it relies on a finding the action that maximizes the action-value function, which in the continuous valued case requires an iterative optimization process at every step.
Such large action spaces are difficult to explore efficiently, and thus successfully training DQN-like networks in this context is likely intractable.
In this work we present a model-free, off-policy actor-critic algorithm using deep function approximators that can learn policies in high-dimensional, continuous action spaces.
In this work we present a model-free, off-policy actor-critic algorithm using deep function approximators that can learn policies in high-dimensional, continuous action spaces.
Our work is based on the deterministic policy gradient (DPG) algorithm (itself similar to NFQCA , and similar ideas can be found in ).
A long-standing challenge of robotic control is to learn an action policy directly from raw sensory input such as video.

A key feature of the approach is its simplicity: it requires only a straightforward actor-critic architecture and learning algorithm with very few "moving parts", making it easy to implement and scale to more difficult problems and larger networks.

A key feature of the approach is its simplicity: it requires only a straightforward actor-critic architecture and learning algorithm with very few "moving parts", making it easy to implement and scale to more difficult problems and larger networks.
Q-learning , a commonly used off-policy algorithm, uses the greedy policy µ(s) = arg max a Q(s, a).
Recently, adapted the Q-learning algorithm in order to make effective use of large neural networks as function approximators.
Their algorithm was able to learn to play Atari games from pixels.
The original DPG paper evaluated the algorithm with toy problems using tile-coding and linear function approximators.
It has often been assumed that standard policy search methods such as those explored in the present work are simply too fragile to scale to difficult problems .
